title: Roggenbrot
subtitle: Dense German rye bread with a sour, earthy flavour
excerpt: A dense, close-crumbed German rye loaf leavened entirely with sourdough starter. The high rye content makes the dough behave more like a stiff batter than a bread dough: it cannot be kneaded in the usual sense and is baked in a tin to hold its shape. The finished loaf is moist and slightly sticky, with a deeply sour flavour from the long fermentation, and slices cleanly without crumbling.
sourceNotes: Roggenbrot (rye bread) is among the oldest documented breads in the German-speaking world, with references in German agricultural and domestic records from the medieval period onward. Friedrich Küchenmeisterei (1485, public domain) describes rye-based breads as a staple in northern and central European households. The sourdough-leavened, tin-baked high-rye format is described in German domestic cookery and baking manuals published before 1928, including Henriette Davidis, Praktisches Kochbuch (1847), a nineteenth-century German culinary standard now freely available in digitised editions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice before freezing. Rye bread is best eaten one or two days after baking once the crumb has set; freeze day-two slices for best results.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rest the baked loaf, wrapped, for at least 24 hours before slicing. The crumb firms and the sour flavour mellows. Freshly baked roggenbrot slices too stickily.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven, starting high to set the crust, then reducing to 200°C after 15 minutes. Fan at 210°C reducing to 180°C. Total bake time 55 to 65 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rye flour contains very little gluten-forming protein and high levels of pentosans (complex carbohydrates) that absorb large amounts of water. A high-rye dough is sticky, extensible rather than elastic, and cannot be kneaded or shaped like a wheat dough. It is mixed to a thick, cohesive paste, transferred directly to a well-oiled tin with wet or oiled hands, and smoothed level with a wet spatula or bench scraper. Any attempt to flour and knead it will result in a stiff, crumbly dough that tears during shaping.

content.text: Rye bread is one of the easier sourdough breads in terms of daily effort, but requires planning: the levain needs building the day before, and the baked loaf needs 24 hours to rest before it slices cleanly.
Rye dough behaves like a thick batter
rather than a standard bread dough, and is transferred straight into the tin rather than shaped. Work with wet or oiled hands and tools throughout.

Eight hours before mixing, combine 50 g of your rye starter with 75 g rye flour and 75 g warm water. Stir well, cover, and leave at room temperature until visibly bubbly and domed. This 200 g levain will carry the fermentation for the whole loaf.

Combine the rye flour, salt, and caraway seeds in a large bowl. Pour in the warm water and add the levain. Mix vigorously with a stiff spatula for 3 to 4 minutes until everything is thoroughly combined and the dough forms a sticky, dense paste. Cover tightly and leave at room temperature for 2 hours.

Oil a 900 g loaf tin generously. Scrape the dough into the tin with wet hands and smooth the surface level with a wet spatula. Dust the top with a little rye flour. Cover loosely and prove for 60 minutes at room temperature until the dough has risen slightly and surface cracks appear. Heat the oven to 230°C conventional (210°C fan). Bake for 15 minutes, then reduce to 200°C conventional (180°C fan) and bake for a further 45 to 50 minutes until the loaf sounds hollow when tapped from the tin and reads 96°C in the centre. Turn out and cool on a rack. Wrap in a cloth or paper bag and rest for 24 hours before slicing.
